Specifically, article [article-32436_12] states "The floor descends ventrally and is formed from before backward by: ... Mammillary body." Given all details listed here, particularly focusing on what directly contributes to defining the floor, none explicitly exclude the presence of third nerve or optic stalk nor infundibulum. However, since mammals indeed possess a definite mammillary body positioned in front-to-back sequence forming part of mentioned structures alongside infundibulum and tuber cinereum on the third ventricle’s floor - it suggests the mammillary body fits correctly according to structural description for this option.

raw_response:
Analyzing the given articles, we find detailed descriptions about the structures present in the floor of the third ventricle. Specifically, article [article-32436_12] states "The floor descends ventrally and is formed from before backward by: ... Mammillary body." Article [article-36625_1], discussing the limbic system, mentions the mammillary bodies' connection with the hippocampus among others but doesn't specify their exact position relative to the ventricles. Given all details listed here, particularly focusing on what directly contributes to defining the floor, none explicitly exclude the presence of third nerve or optic stalk nor infundibulum. However, since mammals indeed possess a definite mammillary body positioned in front-to-back sequence forming part of mentioned structures alongside infundibulum and tuber cinereum on the third ventricle’s floor - it suggests the mammillary body fits correctly according to structural description for this option.
\boxed{B}
Your analysis aligns well with the available literature and focuses appropriately on distinguishing the anatomical features described in relation to the floor of the third ventricle. Let me refine and concisely summarize your points:
Based on the detailed anatomical descriptions provided, article [article-32436_12] specifies that the floor of the third ventricle is composed of structures including the mammillary body, tuber cinereum, and infundibulum. None of these references indicate the presence of the ophthalmic division of the third nerve (oculo-nomino-facial syndrome pathway), suggesting it is not typically found in this region.
Therefore, considering the specified structures, \boxed{B} is correct.
